facts.claim: Crane is found dead at the foot of the iron stair.
facts.quote: Crane had fallen from the top.

facts.claim: Crane the director was blind for two years.
facts.quote: the man had been blind these two years

facts.claim: A half-burnt telegram form lies unread in the dead grate.
facts.quote: the printed banding of a telegraph form and three or four words

facts.claim: Hale writes the death down as a fall.
facts.quote: the adjuster wrote fall in the first clean line of his book

facts.claim: Junia means to claim she worked at the spectroscope all night.
facts.quote: She had been at the spectroscope, she would say. All night

facts.claim: Junia was on the dome gallery in the night, pressed to the wall.
facts.quote: she had stood pressed to the curve of the wall with her breath held

facts.claim: Junia saw who climbed the stair that night.
facts.quote: She had seen who climbed the stair.

facts.claim: The night-log's final entry is in a hand other than Crane's.
facts.quote: the hand was wrong

facts.claim: Hale suspects a second presence and writes 'Who?'
facts.quote: he wrote a single word and underlined it: Who?

facts.claim: The plate in the camera is blank, never exposed to the sky.
facts.quote: the plate was blank — clean grey gelatine, never drawn back to the sky

facts.claim: Hale lets Junia stand as a witness and not a suspect.
facts.quote: let her stand, in his book, as a witness and not a suspect

facts.claim: The meridian clock is stopped at fourteen minutes past three.
facts.quote: Fourteen minutes past three. The hands held it there

facts.claim: The clock had been wound only hours before it stopped.
facts.quote: the clock had been wound, and wound recently, only hours before it stopped

facts.claim: The clock-case is bolted into the same iron framing as the foot of the stair.
facts.quote: pier and stair and clock-case all married into one rigid skeleton of metal

facts.claim: The instrument account has a soft place where figures do not carry.
facts.quote: a place where the figures did not quite carry

facts.claim: Crane wrote a resignation in his own hand, never sent, abandoned in the desk.
facts.quote: A resignation. The director's own, private ... set down and not sent.

facts.claim: Pike goes white when he sees Hale reading the unsent letter.
facts.quote: went the colour of the snow at the window, and turned away

facts.claim: The spare dome key is reported lost a month before, off the board.
facts.quote: spare dome key mislaid, to replace

facts.claim: The spare key opens the lower dome door at the foot of the iron stair.
facts.quote: A key to the lower dome door, the door that gave on the foot of the iron stair

facts.claim: The instrument account has been bled of money over two years.
facts.quote: the account had been bled

facts.claim: Hale sees a motive lying open beneath his palm.
facts.quote: there was a motive lying open on the page beneath his palm

facts.claim: Pike confesses he took the money guinea by guinea.
facts.quote: He had taken the money.

facts.claim: Pike confesses he forged the patron's recall telegram and sent it up.
facts.quote: I wrote it. I forged the patron's hand and sent it up

facts.claim: Hale concludes Pike was the cause of the death, not accident.
facts.quote: The bursar was the cause of the death.

facts.claim: Hale resolves to find the telegram whole down the hill.
facts.quote: In the morning, he decided, he would go down the hill and find it whole.

facts.claim: The recall telegram lies undelivered in the post-box, never carried to the dome.
facts.quote: franked and ready and undelivered, snowed in a clean month

facts.claim: Crane never held nor read the recall.
facts.quote: Crane had never held it. Crane had never read a word of it.

facts.claim: Hale's certainty of Pike's guilt cracks across.
facts.quote: felt his hard certainty of the night before crack straight across

facts.claim: Pike believes himself a murderer, sure he sent the wire up.
facts.quote: in his own mind, a murderer

facts.claim: Pike kept the key warm in his coat-lining the whole month.
facts.quote: the key warm in his coat-lining all this month

facts.claim: Hale returns to the dome to re-read each thing he left.
facts.quote: to go back to each thing he had been content to leave

facts.claim: The developed plate shows nothing; not one second of sky touched it.
facts.quote: not one second of the night's sky had ever touched this glass

facts.claim: Hale concludes Junia was not at the instrument; the witness turns liar.
facts.quote: the woman had not been at the instrument

facts.claim: The clock was struck by the shock of the fall, recording the instant 3:14.
facts.quote: The clock had been struck. ... it was the recorded instant of the fall itself

facts.claim: The strange log hand is Junia's, writing the figures at blind Crane's dictation.
facts.quote: The log is my hand ... he would dictate, and I would write the figures down for him

facts.claim: Hale sets the log down as innocent; no forger, no second watcher.
facts.quote: The log was innocent.

facts.claim: Junia names Pike as the shape that climbed the iron stair.
facts.quote: It was Pike, she said. On the stair.

facts.claim: Pike came in by the lower door with a key Junia had locked.
facts.quote: He came in by the lower door — he had a key

facts.claim: Crane was already on the stair; Pike never laid a hand on him; no murder.
facts.quote: Pike never laid a hand on him. ... it was no murder.

facts.claim: The key was never mislaid; Pike took it into his coat the day he reported it lost.
facts.quote: It had never been mislaid. ... gone off the board into the lining of the bursar's coat

facts.claim: With the key in place the blank plate, struck clock and undelivered wire fall true for Hale.
facts.quote: with the key in its place the rest fell true behind it

facts.claim: Hale certifies a fall occasioned by unlawful intrusion, not accidental, no malice.
facts.quote: a fall, occasioned by unlawful intrusion upon the deceased; not accidental within the meaning of the policy

facts.claim: Junia feeds the night-log to the stove and it burns to nothing.
facts.quote: she had put the log in. ... curl and blacken and fall to nothing

facts.claim: The summit keeps the climber's name; the one record that could speak is ash.
facts.quote: the summit would keep him now

facts.claim: Hale certifies death by fall, cause undetermined, no unlawful act established; policy pays.
facts.quote: death by fall; cause undetermined; no unlawful act against the deceased established

facts.claim: Hale says nothing and turns back to the ledgers to reconstruct the theft.
facts.quote: he closed his own book softly and turned back to the ledgers, and said nothing at all

facts.claim: Hale reconstructs the whole theft from the page, leaving Pike unsuspected.
facts.quote: He built the whole drained shape of it on his own clean page

facts.claim: Pike comes out in the dark carrying duplicate books, patting something at his lining.
facts.quote: carrying something against his chest ... something smaller that the man kept patting at through his coat

facts.claim: Pike draws the 'lost' key from his coat-lining and unlocks the lower dome.
facts.quote: drew out a key. ... The lost key was not lost.

facts.claim: Seeing the hidden key, Hale sets down that the accident was no accident.
facts.quote: the accident was no accident

facts.claim: Pike buries the duplicate books in the dome foundations in the dark.
facts.quote: the rustle of the duplicate books going down into the dark of the foundations

facts.claim: Hale holds the whole proved case in his head, every line carried to a thing seen.
facts.quote: held the whole of it in his head, complete

facts.claim: Hale develops the plate alone; nothing came, the alibi breaks.
facts.quote: Not one second of sky had ever touched it.

facts.claim: The witness turns liar in Hale's book; she was elsewhere in the dome.
facts.quote: the witness he had let stand turned, in his book, into the liar

facts.claim: The clock was struck through the shared frame; 3:14 is the instant of the fall.
facts.quote: 3:14 was no planted alibi but the recorded instant of the fall itself

facts.claim: Hale reasons the strange hand was Junia's kindness at blind Crane's dictation.
facts.quote: someone had stood at his elbow and taken them down at his dictation

facts.claim: The forged wire lies undelivered and snowed-in at the post-box; Crane never read it.
facts.quote: franked and snowed-in and undelivered ... Crane had never held it.

facts.claim: The unsent resignation shows Crane meant to leave of his own grief.
facts.quote: The old astronomer had meant to lay down his post — of his own grief

facts.claim: Hale certifies a fall occasioned by unlawful unobserved intrusion, not accident, no malice.
facts.quote: a fall, occasioned by the deceased being startled by an unlawful and unobserved intrusion
